title: Self-evolving personal-agent persona — research brief + prototype spec
subtitle: Wenxuan's open question: 'I hope every personal agent could have a real-time, self-evolving persona, so I won't face tedious text to do brief everyday.' This is the first pass at turning that into a buildable thing.

blocks.label: The one-line thesis
blocks.text: A personal agent that can tell me who I am *today* — without me re-explaining — is more valuable than one that can do one more tool call. Persona is the compounding moat of a personal AI.

blocks.markdown:
## The problem Wenxuan stated

> 'I hope every personal agent could have a real-time persona, self-evolving, so I won't face tedious text to do brief every day.'

Unpacked:

1. **Real-time** — persona reflects the last hour, not the last quarter. When Wenxuan ships a PR, the persona should know *in the next conversation* that he just shipped a PR.
2. **Self-evolving** — no manual prompt editing; the agent updates its own persona from observed signals.
3. **No tedious briefing** — the user should never have to restate context that the agent should already have learned.

## What this is NOT

- Not a chatbot that pretends to be a personality (role-play personas)
- Not a static system prompt that gets written once
- Not a RAG-over-chat-history — retrieval-on-demand isn't the same as an evolving self-model

## Prior art — what exists in 2026

### Research I found (arxiv 2026-04)

| Paper | Takeaway |
|---|---|
| **FileGram** (2604.04901) | Grounds agent personalization in *file-system behavioral traces* — the files you touch, when, and how, are a high-signal personalization substrate. No explicit user input needed. Directly relevant. |
| **EvolvR** (self-evolving pairwise reasoning) | Multi-persona self-synthesis for judge training. Peripheral, but confirms 'self-evolving' is a live research direction in 2026. |
| **MemGPT / Letta** (2023, still the reference arch) | Agent with tiered memory (context window = RAM, archival = disk) and self-paging. First production system to separate 'working persona' from 'long-term observations'. |
| **Generative Agents** (Park et al., 2023) | Observation → reflection → memory stream. The *reflection* step is where persona condensation happens. |
| **Voyager** (Wang et al., 2023) | Lifelong learning via skill library, not persona — but the 'append & self-edit' pattern generalizes. |

### Industry state (2026-Q1/Q2)

- **ChatGPT Memory** (OpenAI) — opaque, user-invisible, no write API. Feels like the MVP of what Wenxuan is asking for, but you can't inspect or edit it.
- **Claude Projects** (Anthropic) — project-level instructions but no auto-evolution.
- **Cursor Rules / `AGENTS.md`** (2025) — static per-repo prompts, explicitly manual.
- **Hermes (this stack)** — already has `memory`, `user`, `skills` partitions. Closest to the target architecture, but the evolution step is still agent-initiated (I add a memory when I notice something), not continuous.

## The design gap I see

Most 'memory' systems store **events**. A persona needs to store **identity hypotheses**.

| Event memory | Persona memory |
|---|---|
| 'User merged PR #1 in BedRock at 14:22' | 'User ships end-to-end features when solo, prefers draft PRs + diff review when collaborative.' |
| 'User paused 3 times in conversation' | 'User's decision style: asks clarifying questions when the option space is >3.' |
| 'User switches CN/EN depending on topic' | 'User thinks in CN for product strategy, EN for engineering. Responds faster when matched.' |

Event memory is **episodic** (facts, timestamped). Persona memory is **semantic** (claims about the self).

The evolution step = periodically distill episodic observations into updated semantic claims, with provenance and decay.

## Proposed architecture — 'Persona Core'

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     OBSERVATION LAYER                    │
│  Chat transcripts · Commits · Calendar · Files touched   │
│  Feishu messages · Schedule adherence · Decision timings │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │  (always-on signal ingestion)
                       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   EPISODIC STORE                         │
│  JSONL: {ts, source, type, payload, salience_score}     │
│  Never rewritten. Source of truth for the evolution.    │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │  (hourly / daily distillation)
                       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 PERSONA CORE (semantic)                  │
│  Claims: {id, text, support_count, last_evidence_ts,    │
│           confidence, contradicts_ids[]}                 │
│  - Preferences                                           │
│  - Working style                                         │
│  - Current projects & priorities                         │
│  - Skills & knowledge state                              │
│  - Recent state (what happened this week)                │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                       │  (injected every turn, 500-800 tokens)
                       ▼
                  [SYSTEM PROMPT]
```

### The 3 evolution rules

1. **Support accumulates.** Every new observation that matches an existing claim bumps its `support_count` and `last_evidence_ts`.
2. **Contradictions fork, not overwrite.** New evidence that contradicts a claim creates a sibling claim; confidence on the original decays. Both stay visible until one wins on support.
3. **Claims expire on silence.** If `last_evidence_ts > 30d`, confidence decays. Low-confidence claims get moved to cold storage, not deleted.

### The distillation loop (the 'self-evolving' bit)

Runs on a schedule (every N minutes / on event boundary). Prompt:

> Given these new observations since T_last and the current persona core, propose:
> (a) new claims, (b) claims to strengthen, (c) claims to contradict. Return as a diff.

LLM produces a structured diff → apply → write back. Agent's next turn reads the updated core.

## Wenxuan's stack already has most of this

- **`memory` store** — the episodic substrate. Already capped, already append-only-ish.
- **`user` store** — the *start* of a persona core, but it's hand-edited and not auto-evolving.
- **`session_search`** — the bridge from episodic to semantic; the agent already uses this.
- **`skill_manage`** — procedural memory. Separate axis (not persona), already working.

**What's missing:**

1. An **observation ingestor** that writes structured events to a durable episodic log (not just chat transcripts).
2. A **distillation cron** that runs hourly and updates `user` from episodic → semantic.
3. A **persona viewer** (dashboard inner page) so Wenxuan can read what the agent thinks of him, correct it, and watch it evolve.

## Prototype scope (v0 — buildable this week)

| Component | Where | Effort |
|---|---|---|
| Event log | `~/.hermes/persona/episodic.jsonl` | 1 h |
| Ingestor hooks | Existing agent lifecycle + `data/agent-journal.jsonl` feed | 2 h |
| Nightly distillation cron | Reuses `wenxuan-overnight-workflow` skill, runs at 23:50 | 2 h |
| Persona viewer | `/persona` inner page on the morning brief dashboard, reads `~/.hermes/persona/claims.json` | 3 h |
| Correction UI | One-click 'this claim is wrong' on the viewer; writes a refutation event | 2 h |
| Inject into system prompt | Via `memory` tool at session start | 0 h (free, already done) |

**Total: ~10 hours. Achievable in a single weekend.**

## Risks & open questions

### Privacy
Every signal the ingestor captures is a personal datapoint. File-system traces (à la FileGram) are the highest-signal, highest-risk source. **Decision needed:** what sources are in-scope for v0? (Safe default: chat + commits + morning-brief decisions only.)

### Drift
LLM distillation will hallucinate claims. Mitigations: keep provenance (cite the 3+ observations that support each claim), allow user refutation, cap core size at ~50 claims.

### The 'horror' failure mode
Imagine the agent proposes: 'User is easily frustrated when...' — and it's right, but seeing it written out is upsetting. **Rule:** persona claims should be framed in terms of *preferences and working styles*, not character judgments. This is a prompt-engineering problem, solvable with rubric.

### Who owns the core?
If persona lives in `~/.hermes/persona/`, it's local. If we sync to cloud, it becomes portable across devices but needs encryption. v0 = local-only.

### Does this actually reduce 'tedious briefing'?
Only if the persona core is *actually injected* every turn and is *actually relevant* to the task. Metric: count of '(I already told you this)' corrections per week. Target: trend to zero over 30 days.

blocks.label: Decision I need from Wenxuan
blocks.text: Do we prototype this? If yes: (a) ship a minimal v0 this weekend, (b) scope it to chat+commits+journal only for privacy, (c) expose via /persona page for live inspection. If no: I'll keep this as a design doc and revisit when the dashboard work stabilizes.

items: Get Wenxuan's go/no-go on prototype scope
Draft the distillation prompt + eval cases (dogfood on own chat history first)
Decide: 50-claim cap, or unbounded with decay?
Design the /persona inner page — read-only vs. editable-with-provenance
How does this interact with the existing 'user' memory partition? Replace? Augment?
Write the initial observations schema (event types, salience scoring)
